title: Municipal corporation

infobox.value: is the legal term for a [local governing body](/wiki/Local_government "Local government"), including (but not necessarily limited to) [cities](/wiki/City "City"), [counties](/wiki/County "County"), [towns](/wiki/Town "Town"), [townships](/wiki/Township "Township"), [charter townships](/wiki/Charter_township "Charter township"), [villages](/wiki/Village "Village"), and [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough").[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1) The term can also be used to describe [municipally owned corporations](/wiki/Municipally_owned_corporation "Municipally owned corporation").[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1)[[2]](#cite_note-TavaresCamoes-2)[[3]](#cite_note-GrossiReichard-3)

or **town charter** or **municipal charter** is a [legal document](/wiki/Legal_document "Legal document") establishing a municipality, such as a [city](/wiki/City "City") or [town](/wiki/Town "Town").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Legal term for a local governing body

**Municipal corporation** is the legal term for a [local governing body](/wiki/Local_government "Local government"), including (but not necessarily limited to) [cities](/wiki/City "City"), [counties](/wiki/County "County"), [towns](/wiki/Town "Town"), [townships](/wiki/Township "Township"), [charter townships](/wiki/Charter_township "Charter township"), [villages](/wiki/Village "Village"), and [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough").[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1) The term can also be used to describe [municipally owned corporations](/wiki/Municipally_owned_corporation "Municipally owned corporation").[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1)[[2]](#cite_note-TavaresCamoes-2)[[3]](#cite_note-GrossiReichard-3)

## Municipal corporation as local self-government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Municipal corporation as local self-government")]

Municipal incorporation occurs when such [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") become self-governing entities under the laws of the state or province in which they are located. Often, this event is marked by the award or declaration of a [municipal charter](/wiki/Municipal_charter "Municipal charter"). A **city charter** or **town charter** or **municipal charter** is a [legal document](/wiki/Legal_document "Legal document") establishing a municipality, such as a [city](/wiki/City "City") or [town](/wiki/Town "Town").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

Main article: [List of city corporations in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_city_corporations_in_Bangladesh "List of city corporations in Bangladesh")

Main article: [List of municipal corporations in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_municipal_corporations_in_Bangladesh "List of municipal corporations in Bangladesh")

There are 12 city corporations in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"). Two of them are located in the capital [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") and the remaining 10 are located in the most populous [cities](/wiki/Cities "Cities") of the eight divisions.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] They carry out major works in the cities and perform socio-economic and civic functions. In addition, there are 330 municipalities in the eight [divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"). A city corporation is a much stronger and larger [Local governing body](/wiki/Local_governing_body "Local governing body") than a municipal corporation. This is because a city corporation consists of a [metropolitan city](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality "Metropolitan municipality") of a [district](/wiki/District "District") and a municipal corporation consists of a [municipal](/wiki/Municipal "Municipal") area in a [sub-district](/wiki/Upazila "Upazila").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Municipal government in Canada](/wiki/Municipal_government_in_Canada "Municipal government in Canada")

In Canada, charters are granted by [provincial authorities](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada").

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: India")]

Main article: [Municipal corporation (India)](/wiki/Municipal_corporation_(India) "Municipal corporation (India)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Ripon_Building_panorama.jpg/250px-Ripon_Building_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ripon_Building_panorama.jpg)

[Chennai Corporation](/wiki/Chennai_Corporation "Chennai Corporation") headquarters

Municipal corporations are the local bodies responsible for governing large urban areas in India. Their establishment is mandated by the [74th amendment](/wiki/Seventy-fourth_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Seventy-fourth Amendment of the Constitution of India"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) and each municipal corporation is established through and governed by municipal acts passed by [state legislatures](/wiki/State_legislative_assemblies_of_India "State legislative assemblies of India").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The [Greater Chennai Corporation](/wiki/Greater_Chennai_Corporation "Greater Chennai Corporation") is the oldest municipal corporation in India.[[8]](#cite_note-oldestcorp-8)

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ireland")]

The title "corporation" was used in [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough") from soon after the [Norman conquest](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_Ireland "Norman conquest of Ireland") until the [Local Government Act 2001](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_2001 "Local Government Act 2001"). Under the 2001 act, [county boroughs](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") were renamed "cities" and their corporations became "city councils"; other borough corporations were renamed "borough councils".[[9]](#cite_note-9)

After the [Partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland"), the corporations in the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") were Dublin, [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"), [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick") and [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford "Waterford") (county boroughs) and [Drogheda](/wiki/Drogheda "Drogheda"), [Kilkenny](/wiki/Kilkenny "Kilkenny"), [Sligo](/wiki/Sligo "Sligo"), [Clonmel](/wiki/Clonmel "Clonmel"), and Wexford (non-county boroughs). [Dún Laoghaire](/wiki/D%C3%BAn_Laoghaire "Dún Laoghaire") gained borough status in 1930 as "The Corporation of Dun Laoghaire".[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway")'s borough status, lost in 1840, was restored in 1937; it was formally styled "the Mayor, Aldermen and Burgesses of the Borough of Galway",[[11]](#cite_note-11) but referred to as "the Corporation".[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

The [New Zealand Constitution Act 1852](/wiki/New_Zealand_Constitution_Act_1852 "New Zealand Constitution Act 1852") allowed municipal corporations to be established within the new [provinces of New Zealand](/wiki/Provinces_of_New_Zealand "Provinces of New Zealand"). The term fell out of favour following the abolition of the provinces in 1876.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Main article: [Municipal Corporation (Pakistan)](/wiki/Municipal_Corporation_(Pakistan) "Municipal Corporation (Pakistan)")

Municipal corporations are local government bodies responsible for running the city system of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). They have been established under their respective [Local Government Acts](/wiki/Local_government_in_Pakistan "Local government in Pakistan") of the provinces.
Article 140-A of the [Constitution of Pakistan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan"), introduced by the
18th Amendment in 2010, mandates that each province must establish a local government system by law and devolve political, administrative, and financial power to elected local representatives.

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [Borough status in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom")

The [ancient boroughs](/wiki/Ancient_borough "Ancient borough") of [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") were typically incorporated by a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter"), though some were boroughs by [prescription](/wiki/Customary_law "Customary law"). The [Municipal Corporations Act 1835](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1835 "Municipal Corporations Act 1835") and [Municipal Corporations Act 1882](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1882 "Municipal Corporations Act 1882") abolished the corporations of [rotten boroughs](/wiki/Rotten_borough "Rotten borough") and other small rural areas. The [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888") aligned the powers of the remaining borough corporations with those of the new [urban district councils](/wiki/Urban_district_(England_and_Wales) "Urban district (England and Wales)"). All borough corporations were replaced under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972") with councils not designated as "corporations", with the exception of the [City of London Corporation](/wiki/City_of_London_Corporation "City of London Corporation").

The corporations of the [burghs](/wiki/Burgh "Burgh") of Scotland were similar in origin and were reformed or replaced in the nineteenth century before being abolished by the [Local Government (Scotland) Act 1973](/wiki/Local_Government_(Scotland)_Act_1973 "Local Government (Scotland) Act 1973"). The Irish borough corporations within what is now [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") were reformed by the [Municipal Corporations (Ireland) Act 1840](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_(Ireland)_Act_1840 "Municipal Corporations (Ireland) Act 1840") and [Local Government (Ireland) Act 1898](/wiki/Local_Government_(Ireland)_Act_1898 "Local Government (Ireland) Act 1898") and replaced by the [Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_(Northern_Ireland)_1972 "Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 1972").

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Local government in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States")

Most [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") and territories have at least two tiers of local government: [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") and [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"). [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") uses the term [parish](/wiki/List_of_parishes_in_Louisiana "List of parishes in Louisiana"), [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") uses the term [borough](/wiki/List_of_boroughs_and_census_areas_in_Alaska "List of boroughs and census areas in Alaska"), and [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") uses the term [planning region](/wiki/Councils_of_governments_in_Connecticut "Councils of governments in Connecticut") for what the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") terms [county equivalents](/wiki/County_equivalent "County equivalent") in those states. [Civil townships](/wiki/Civil_township "Civil township") or towns are used as subdivisions of a county in 20 states, mostly in the Northeast and Midwest.

Population centers may be organized into incorporated municipalities of several types, including the [city](/wiki/City "City"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), [borough](/wiki/Borough_(United_States) "Borough (United States)"), and [village](/wiki/Village_(United_States) "Village (United States)"). The types and nature of these municipal entities vary from state to state. In addition to these general-purpose local governments, states may also create [special-purpose](/wiki/Special_district_(United_States) "Special district (United States)") local governments. Depending on the state, local governments may operate under their own charters or under general law, or a state may have a mix of chartered and general-law local governments. Generally, in a state having both chartered and general-law local governments, the chartered local governments have more local autonomy and home rule. Municipalities are typically subordinate to a county government, with some exceptions. Certain cities, for example, have consolidated with their county government as [consolidated city-counties](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county"). In [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), cities are completely [independent](/wiki/Independent_city_(United_States) "Independent city (United States)") from the county in which they would otherwise be a part. In some states, particularly in New England, [towns](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") form the primary unit of local government below the state level, in some cases eliminating the need for county government entirely. Many rural areas and even some suburban areas of many states have no municipal government below the county level.

In addition to counties and municipalities, states often create special purpose authorities, such as [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district") and districts for fire protection, [sanitary sewer](/wiki/Sanitary_sewer "Sanitary sewer") service, [public transportation](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport"), [public libraries](/wiki/Public_library "Public library"), public parks or forests, water resource management, and [conservation districts](/wiki/Conservation_district "Conservation district"). Such special purpose districts may encompass areas in multiple municipalities or counties. According to the US Census Bureau's data collected in 2012, there were 89,004 local government units in the United States. This data shows a decline from 89,476 units since the last census of local governments performed in 2007.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Each of the five permanently inhabited [U.S. territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States") is also subdivided into smaller entities. Puerto Rico has [78 municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Puerto_Rico "Municipalities of Puerto Rico"), and the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands") has four municipalities. [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam") has [villages](/wiki/List_of_populated_places_in_Guam#List_of_villages "List of populated places in Guam"), the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/United_States_Virgin_Islands "United States Virgin Islands") has [districts](/wiki/Districts_and_sub-districts_of_the_United_States_Virgin_Islands "Districts and sub-districts of the United States Virgin Islands"), and [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa") has [districts and unorganized atolls](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_American_Samoa "Administrative divisions of American Samoa").

Each [Indian Reservation](/wiki/Indian_Reservation "Indian Reservation") is subdivided in various ways. For example, the [Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") is subdivided into agencies and [chapter houses](/wiki/Chapter_house_(Navajo_Nation) "Chapter house (Navajo Nation)"), while the [Blackfeet Nation](/wiki/Blackfeet_Indian_Reservation "Blackfeet Indian Reservation") is subdivided into [communities](/wiki/Blackfeet_Indian_Reservation "Blackfeet Indian Reservation").

## Municipal corporations as enterprises

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_corporation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Municipal corporations as enterprises")]

According to one definition of the term, municipal corporations are "organisations with independent corporate status, managed by an executive board appointed primarily by local government officials, and with majority public ownership".[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1) Some such corporations rely on revenue from user fees, distinguishing them from agencies and special districts funded through taxation,[[2]](#cite_note-TavaresCamoes-2) although this is not always the case.[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1) Such municipal corporations result from a process of "externalization", and require different skills and orientations from the respective local governments, and follow common changes[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the institutional landscape of public services.[[3]](#cite_note-GrossiReichard-3) They are argued to be more efficient than government bureaucracies, having been observed as generally more successful in providing most public goods and services, despite seeming to have higher failure rates initially, hypothetically because of their legal and managerial status.[[1]](#cite_note-Voornetal-1)
